Fatma Kamal is a Sr. Network Engineer with contact information including email, phone number, GitHub, LinkedIn, and a personal website.
Fatma Kamal obtained a M.Sc. from AISECT University in 2018 in Cairo, Egypt, and a B. Com from Jiwaji University in 2015 in Gwalior, India.

From January 2020 to the present, Fatma Kamal worked as a Sr. Network Engineer at Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd. in Cairo, Egypt, where she led router, switch, and server configurations, oversaw earthing and trenching, and managed backup schedules.

As a Sr. Network Engineer at Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd., Fatma Kamal implemented plans for railway station WiFi environments, administered Windows Servers (2008 R2, 2012 R2, 2016), and provided troubleshooting for LAN & WAN infrastructure.

From September 2018 to December 2019, Fatma Kamal worked as an IT Executive at Asha MMPC in Bali, Rajasthan, India, managing backups, administering Windows Servers, and troubleshooting network infrastructure.

The individual configured networking devices like Cisco Routers and switches, conducted health checks on File and Application Servers, and maintained Windows 7 Professional networks, including network printers.
The individual installed server-side software, managed user accounts and assigned rights on shared drives.

As a Network Support Engineer at Interface Techno System from June 2015 to December 2016 in Bhind, India, the individual implemented and maintained backup schedules, upgraded Cisco router configurations, and administered various Windows Servers and Linux OS.
The individual troubleshooted LAN infrastructure, configured Cisco networking devices, managed Check Point Endpoint Security, and performed regular health checks on servers.

The individual logged and monitored calls using CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager, managed Windows XP Professional networks, and issued E Tokens for laptop users, managing over 350 Desktops and Laptops.
The individual executed backups using Symantec Backup Exec 12D, restored files, handled escalations, generated reports, and prepared MIS, vendor, and inventory management reports.

As a System Engineer at Nava Bharat Press (P) Ltd from March 2014 to May 2015 in Cairo, Egypt, the individual managed servers, backups, and provided helpdesk support, while monitoring Service Level Agreements (SLA).
The individual hosted client meetings, performed system management and troubleshooting, and provided hardware troubleshooting and support.
The individual holds Tally Certification from Tally Academy, and has training in Manual and Computerized Accounting with Tally ERP9.

The individual possesses skills in various Operating Systems (Windows, Linux, Ubuntu, RHEL 6), Networking Technologies (Cisco Routing and Switching, InterVLAN Routing, etc.), and Networking Devices (Cisco Routers, Switches, HP ProLiant Servers, etc.).

The text lists several directory services including Active Directory (AD) and Read-Only Domain Controller (RODC), along with file transfer protocol (FTP), print servers, System Center Configuration Manager (SCCM), Windows Deployment Services (WDS), Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP), Domain Name System (DNS), Remote Installation Services (RIS), Group Policies, User Policies, and Computer Policies.
The text details backup technologies such as Server 2016 backup tools and Symantec Backup EXEC 12D.
The text mentions virtualization technologies including VMware ESXi, VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, and GNS3 Network Simulator.
The text lists other tools and concepts such as CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager, Check Point Endpoint Security MI Management Console, and Tally ERP9.
From January 2017 to August 2018, Fatma Kamal was a Network Engineer at Interface Techno System in Gwalior, India, implementing backups and administering Windows and Linux servers.

Fatma Kamal is a seasoned Senior Network Engineer, a meticulous architect of connectivity with a global perspective forged through experience in both India and Egypt. Holding a Master of Science from AISECT University (2018) and a Bachelor of Commerce from Jiwaji University (2015), she blends a strong academic foundation with practical expertise. 

Currently at Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd. in Cairo, since 2020, Fatma leads the configuration of routers, switches, and servers, ensuring robust and reliable network infrastructure. Her responsibilities extend beyond the digital realm, encompassing the practicalities of earthing and trenching, and the critical task of managing data backup schedules. She’s demonstrated a particular aptitude for large-scale projects, notably implementing WiFi solutions for railway stations through Railwire/Railtel Corporation of India Ltd., and expertly administering Windows Servers (2008 R2, 2012 R2, and 2016). A skilled troubleshooter, she maintains the health of both LAN and WAN infrastructures.

Prior to Cloudatix, Fatma honed her skills as an IT Executive at Asha MMPC in Rajasthan, India (2018-2019), where she managed backups and server administration. Her career began at Interface Techno System in Gwalior, India (2017-2018) as a Network Engineer, gaining foundational experience in backup implementation and both Windows and Linux server administration. 

Fatma is readily accessible via email (fatma.kamal@email.com), phone (+20 101 234 5678), and maintains a professional presence on GitHub (github.com/fatma-kamal) and LinkedIn (linkedin.com/in/fatma-kamal), as well as a personal website (fatmakamal.com). She’s proficient in configuring Cisco routers and switches, and dedicated to ensuring the optimal performance of file and application servers through regular health checks.

Cisco Switches are the workhorses of network infrastructure, reliable and versatile devices found at the heart of countless organizations – and particularly crucial in demanding environments like railway station WiFi deployments. They aren’t glamorous, but they are *essential*, silently directing the flow of data with precision. These aren’t simply boxes with ports; they’re the foundation upon which connectivity is built, capable of sophisticated InterVLAN Routing and forming the backbone of both Local Area Networks (LANs) and Wide Area Networks (WANs). 

A skilled hand is needed to truly unlock their potential. Individuals like Fatma Kamal, a seasoned Network Engineer, understand their intricacies, configuring them to deliver seamless performance. She’s the type who can diagnose a network hiccup and pinpoint the issue to a specific switch, then swiftly implement a solution. While comfortable with a range of networking gear – from HP ProLiant Servers to Cisco Routers – Cisco Switches represent a core competency, a tool she wields with expertise to build and maintain robust, dependable networks. They are, in essence, the silent partners in her work, responding to her commands to keep the digital world moving.

Active Directory is the central nervous system of many Windows-based networks, a meticulously organized digital directory managing users, computers, and resources. It’s not merely a list, but a hierarchical structure – a domain – where everything is categorized and controlled through policies governing user access and computer configurations. Think of it as the gatekeeper, ensuring only authorized personnel gain entry to specific digital spaces. 

Experienced administrators like Fatma Kamal, navigating complex network environments for Cloudatix and Interface Techno System, routinely maintain and troubleshoot these systems, overseeing Windows Servers that *are* Active Directory in practice. It’s a foundational element, working alongside essential services like DHCP and DNS to seamlessly connect users to the network. 

While a full-fledged Active Directory offers comprehensive control, a Read-Only Domain Controller (RODC) provides a secure, limited access point – a specialized variant for environments demanding heightened security. Beyond simple access control, Active Directory empowers centralized software deployment via SCCM and WDS, streamlines system imaging with RIS, and enforces consistent settings through Group, User, and Computer Policies. It’s a powerful, adaptable framework, essential for managing even moderately sized networks, and a cornerstone of modern IT infrastructure.

The Cisco Router stands as a stalwart of network infrastructure, a digital crossroads directing the flow of information. Often found at the heart of both sprawling corporate networks and smaller business setups, it’s a device built for reliable performance and adaptable configuration. It isn’t a flashy piece of technology, preferring quiet competence to overt display. 

Its core function is deceptively simple: to receive data packets and forward them to their correct destination. However, the *how* of that forwarding is where its power lies. Skilled hands – like those of Fatma Kamal, a seasoned Network Engineer who has spent years coaxing optimal performance from these machines – can tailor its configurations to manage complex traffic patterns, prioritize critical data, and ensure network security. 

Kamal’s expertise, honed across roles in Egypt and India, demonstrates the router’s versatility. She’s upgraded its configurations, meticulously planned its placement alongside switches and servers, and ensured its smooth operation through diligent health checks. It’s a device that benefits from careful attention, and Kamal’s work highlights the importance of proactive maintenance – implementing robust backup schedules to safeguard against disruption. 

The Cisco Router doesn’t operate in isolation. It’s frequently found working in concert with Windows Servers, receiving updated configuration files to maintain peak efficiency. It relies on the broader expertise of system and network administrators to fully realize its potential, a collaborative relationship essential for a stable and secure network. Though a silent workhorse, the Cisco Router is a critical component, a foundational element upon which modern connectivity depends.

India is a land steeped in history and burgeoning with modern ambition, a nation where ancient traditions intertwine with a rapidly evolving technological landscape. It’s a place that has nurtured minds like Fatma Kamal, providing the foundational education – a B.Com from Jiwaji University in Gwalior – that launched her into a career spanning network engineering and IT administration. 

The country itself serves as a vital hub for companies like Interface Techno System, a firm where Kamal honed her skills from 2015 to 2018, first as a Network Support Engineer in Bhind, then as a Network Engineer in Gwalior. Here, she became adept at the intricacies of network infrastructure, mastering Cisco configurations, server administration (both Windows and Linux), and the critical task of data backup. 

India’s economic diversity extends to regions like Rajasthan, where Kamal later found herself employed as an IT Executive at Asha MMPC in Bali. This role, from 2018 to 2019, saw her applying her expertise to maintain network stability and manage essential IT systems within a different organizational context. 

Essentially, India isn’t merely a geographical location in Kamal’s story; it’s the bedrock of her professional development, the home to the institutions that educated her and the companies that provided her with practical experience. It’s a nation offering opportunities within a growing IT sector, a place where skilled professionals like Kamal can contribute to and benefit from a dynamic economy. It’s a country that simultaneously holds onto its rich heritage while embracing the future, a duality reflected in the career path of those it nurtures.

Application Servers are vital components of the network infrastructure, quietly humming with the logic that powers applications. They exist in a state of constant monitoring, subject to regular health checks – a practice diligently performed by skilled network engineers like Fatma Kamal. Though often unseen by the end-user, their stability is paramount. File Servers, too, play a role in their wellbeing, proactively assessing their health. These servers aren’t isolated entities; they are integral to a larger system, reliant on a network maintained by professionals ensuring seamless operation. They represent the backend engine, the unseen force delivering functionality, and their consistent performance is a testament to the dedication of those who oversee them. They are the silent workhorses, demanding consistent attention to maintain the flow of data and application access.

Cisco Routers are the workhorses of network infrastructure, reliable and versatile devices found at the heart of countless digital landscapes. They aren’t flashy, but possess a quiet competence – the ability to intelligently direct data packets across networks, ensuring information finds its destination. Often found alongside their sibling, Cisco Switches, and sometimes even HP ProLiant Servers in larger deployments, they are fundamental building blocks for connectivity. 

A skilled hand is needed to truly unlock their potential. Individuals like Fatma Kamal, a Sr. Network Engineer at Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd., demonstrate this mastery. Kamal has directly configured these routers, shaping their behavior to meet specific needs – like establishing robust WiFi environments for bustling railway stations. They respond to a practiced touch, becoming integral to the smooth operation of complex networks, whether supporting a small office or a sprawling public space. While Kamal’s expertise extends to Windows and Linux servers, and a broad range of networking technologies like InterVLAN Routing, Cisco Routers consistently feature in her toolkit, a testament to their enduring relevance in the world of networking. They are, simply put, essential for keeping the digital world moving.

Fatma Kamal is a seasoned Senior Network Engineer, a meticulous architect of connectivity based in Cairo, Egypt. Since January 2020, she’s been a key figure at Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd., where she doesn’t just maintain networks – she builds and fortifies them. Picture her overseeing the practical foundations, from the careful planning of earthing and trenching to the precise configuration of routers, switches, and servers. 

Her expertise extends beyond the hardware; Fatma is adept at administering Windows Server environments – a veteran with experience across 2008 R2, 2012 R2, and 2016. She’s the one called upon when the network falters, a skilled troubleshooter navigating the complexities of both Local Area and Wide Area Networks. But her work isn’t confined to the data center. Fatma has a knack for bringing connectivity to public spaces, notably leading the implementation of WiFi solutions for railway station environments, ensuring seamless access for travelers. She maintains an active online presence, readily connectable through email, phone, GitHub, LinkedIn, and a personal website, a testament to her proactive and engaged approach to her field.

DNS is the silent architect of the network, a foundational service rarely noticed until it falters. It’s the essential translator, converting human-readable domain names – like ‘cloudatix.com’ – into the numerical IP addresses computers use to locate each other. Like a meticulously maintained address book for the internet, DNS ensures traffic flows to the correct destination. 

Fatma Kamal, a seasoned network engineer, understood its critical role, administering it alongside Windows Servers at Cloudatix and Interface Techno System. It wasn’t merely a configuration task; it was ensuring seamless connectivity for environments as diverse as bustling railway station WiFi and complex corporate networks. 

DNS doesn’t operate in isolation. It’s deeply interwoven with the management of entire systems. It’s a key component alongside DHCP, Active Directory, and other directory services, working in concert to define and enforce network policies. Crucially, DNS *manages* Computer Policies, ensuring devices receive the correct configurations and security settings. It’s a quiet authority, dictating how machines behave within a network, a subtle but powerful force in maintaining order and security. Though often unseen, DNS is the bedrock upon which modern network communication is built.

DHCP – the silent orchestrator of network addresses. Not a person, but a protocol, a foundational element within the digital landscapes Fatma Kamal expertly shaped across railway stations and corporate networks. It doesn’t boast flashy interfaces or demand direct attention, yet its presence is *essential*. Think of it as the hotel front desk for devices joining a network – automatically assigning them a temporary room number (an IP address) so they can connect and communicate. 

While Fatma’s hands-on work involved the tangible – configuring Cisco routers, administering Windows Servers – DHCP hummed beneath the surface, a critical component she managed within those systems. It’s a service woven into the fabric of Windows Servers, a responsibility she carried throughout her roles at Interface Techno System and Cloudatix. It doesn’t stand alone; it’s intrinsically linked to the smooth operation of everything else. 

DHCP doesn’t dictate *what* a device can do, but *where* it can be found. And crucially, it works in concert with broader network governance. It’s a partner to Computer Policies, ensuring devices receive not just an address, but also the correct settings and permissions to function within the network’s rules. It’s a quiet power, enabling seamless connectivity and a well-ordered digital environment. It’s the unseen hand that allows countless devices to find their place and play their part in the network’s complex symphony.

Windows Servers are the steadfast workhorses of network infrastructure, quietly enabling connectivity and data flow. Appearing in iterations from 2008 R2 through 2016 and beyond, they are versatile platforms consistently entrusted with critical administrative duties. They aren’t flashy, but they are foundational – the silent organizers of digital space. 

These servers are frequently found at the heart of organizations, diligently managing core network services. They expertly administer Active Directory, the gatekeeper of user access and security, and reliably handle file transfers via FTP. They are the authoritative sources for DNS, translating human-readable addresses into machine-readable instructions, and efficiently distribute IP addresses through DHCP, ensuring seamless network participation. TFTP benefits from their stable platform as well. 

Their upkeep demands a skilled hand, someone like Fatma Kamal, a network professional who has repeatedly taken on the responsibility of their administration across diverse environments – from the bustling connectivity needs of railway stations to the operational demands of various IT infrastructures in India. Kamal’s expertise extends to maintaining backups, ensuring data resilience, and troubleshooting issues that arise within the broader LAN & WAN landscape. They often work in concert with Cisco routers, benefiting from configuration upgrades to optimize performance. Windows Servers aren’t isolated entities; they are integral components of a larger, interconnected system, demanding consistent attention and capable stewardship.

These servers are the quiet workhorses of Cloudatix Network and Nava Bharat Press, diligently storing data and powering operations. At Cloudatix, under the watchful eye of Sr. Network Engineer Fatma Kamal, they are meticulously configured alongside routers and switches, their foundations secured with careful attention to earthing and trenching. Kamal ensures their continued function through rigorously scheduled backups, safeguarding against data loss. Previously, at Nava Bharat Press, these servers were central to maintaining service, directly supporting adherence to Service Level Agreements – a responsibility that included proactive monitoring and responsive helpdesk support. They aren’t glamorous, but they are essential, silently enabling the businesses they serve and relying on consistent management to remain reliable pillars of infrastructure. Their health is directly tied to the expertise of those who maintain them, and their uptime is a key indicator of overall operational success.

File Servers are the quiet workhorses of Cloudatix Network Pvt. Ltd.’s infrastructure, diligently storing and providing access to critical data. Regularly scrutinized by Sr. Network Engineer Fatma Kamal during routine health checks, these servers are essential components of the network’s overall stability. Though unassuming in their function, they aren’t isolated; they, in turn, actively monitor the health of Application Servers, ensuring a seamless flow of information across the entire system. They represent a foundational layer, silently supporting the more visible aspects of the network, and their consistent performance is a testament to the proactive maintenance provided by engineers like Fatma Kamal. They are the unseen guardians of data, a vital link in the chain of connectivity.
